FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=11,875 participants ages 9-10, 21 recruitment sites, NIMH Data Archive DOI 10.15154/1519271.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: correlation and multiple linear regression analyses on ABCD baseline data examining screen time and mental health, behavioral, academic, sleep, and peer outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in PLoS ONE (2021).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Tables 3 and 5 (and supplementary S6-S31) report per-modality standardized betas for social media time and texting time separately as predictors of individual-trait outcomes (externalizing problems, ADHD, academic performance, sleep quantity/quality, peer relationships). E.g., in Table 3, social media β and texting β are each estimated against mental health and behavioral outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple standardized regression coefficients (β), t-statistics, and partial correlations reported throughout Tables 2-5 for screen time types (including social media and texting) predicting individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported throughout (standardized betas, t-statistics, p-values, partial correlations, and effect sizes in main Tables 2-5 and supplementary Tables S6-S31).

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design (ABCD baseline only); FT-EC7 requires longitudinal design.
notes: The STQ composite total screen time bundles TV, videos, video games, texting, social media, and video chat. However, Step 1 is satisfied because per-modality estimates for social media and texting (phone/SM-specific) paired with individual-trait outcomes appear in Tables 3 and 5 and in supplementary tables S6-S31. Paper qualifies.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study pilot substudy (EARS passive sensing), 67 participants from 4 ABCD sites, year 2 follow-up (ages 11-12), ABCD Release 3.0 DOI 10.15154/1519007.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: descriptive statistics, ANOVA, and Pearson correlations on passively sensed smartphone app use data from an ABCD substudy cohort.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in JMIR Mental Health (2021).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: All inferential analyses (ANOVAs and correlations) pair smartphone use variables against demographics (sex, age, income, race/ethnicity, geographic location) or against self/parent-reported smartphone use measures only. The only significant demographic difference found was girls vs. boys in reading app use (F=5.55, P=.02). No estimate pairs a phone/SM variable with an individual-trait-level outcome (mental health, cognitive, behavioral, etc.). Step 2 not needed. Other-side rule: demographic-only on the other side throughout.

notes: This is a feasibility/descriptive paper characterizing passive sensing of smartphone use. All inferential analyses relate smartphone metrics to demographics only; no individual-trait outcome is analyzed. Fails IC2 on the other-side rule.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=11,875, ages 9-11, Release 2.0, 21 data collection sites, NIMH Data Archive DOI 10.15154/1503209.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: negative binomial regression examining combinations of 24-hour movement guideline adherence (physical activity, screen time, sleep) and internalizing/externalizing behaviors.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in Journal of Adolescent Health (2021).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No per-modality phone/SM-specific estimate exists anywhere in the paper. Screen time is always analyzed as part of guideline-combination groups (e.g., ‘meeting screen time + sleep’ vs. ‘meeting none’), never as a continuous phone/SM-specific variable with its own coefficient. Step 2: The recreational screen time composite (Youth Screen Time Survey) includes TV watching, video watching (YouTube), video game playing, texting, social media, and video chat — it bundles TV and video gaming (disqualifying non-phone/non-SM items) with no separate phone/SM breakout anywhere in the paper.

notes: The screen time variable is a guideline-adherence binary (meeting ≤2 hrs/day) derived from a composite that bundles TV, video games, texting, SM, and video chat with no phone/SM breakout. No per-modality estimate for texting, social media, or video chat appears in main or supplementary tables.
